segments.title: Why carriers were a difficult treaty category
segments.summary: Carrier limits were difficult to design because the ships were still experimental and their military value was unsettled in 1921–22.
segments.body: The opening frames aircraft carriers as a particularly awkward Washington Treaty problem. Early carriers were few, experimental, and often incomplete, so negotiators had little reliable evidence for deciding what counted as a carrier or how much capability a tonnage allowance bought. The Royal Navy could classify nearly every carrier before Courageous as experimental, making replacement rules and treaty accounting unusually flexible. The discussion also introduces the tension between conversion allowances and purpose-built ships, because an inherited hull could consume more treaty tonnage for less aviation capacity.

evidence.note: The transcript describes early carriers as experimental and explains the conversion problem.

segments.title: Carrier ratios and Japan’s strategic position
segments.summary: The treaty’s carrier allocation could make Japan feel relatively weaker as carrier aviation became more important.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke compares the original 5:5:3 carrier ratio with the positions of France and Italy. He argues that a numerical compromise that seemed tolerable while carriers were unproven could become politically sharper once naval aviation mattered more. Japan's carrier allocation was smaller than the combined French and Italian allowance even though neither European navy was its principal anticipated opponent. The perceived imbalance helps explain why Japanese planners would shift reconnaissance capacity to cruiser-borne aircraft, preserving scarce carrier tonnage for duties that only carriers could perform.

evidence.note: The ratio comparison links carrier allocations to Japanese reconnaissance and planning choices.

segments.title: The Washington Treaty’s carrier tonnage rules
segments.summary: A close reading of the carrier articles shows how total allowances, the 27,000-ton cap, and conversion exceptions constrained fleet design.
segments.body: This section walks through the treaty’s total carrier allocations, the normal 27,000-ton individual limit, and the exception for two ships of up to 33,000 tons. It shows why two large conversions could consume so much of a national allowance that little room remained for other carriers, especially under Japan's 81,000-ton total. The quoted articles also limited heavy guns while leaving smaller anti-aircraft weapons unrestricted, reflecting uncertainty about whether carriers might still fight more like gun-armed capital ships. Worked examples turn the legal totals into plausible fleets, showing that even Britain and the United States would have little room after building two large ships and two ships at the normal maximum.

evidence.note: The transcript quotes Articles 7, 9, and 10 and works through the fleet consequences of their tonnage limits.

segments.title: The development path from Cuckoo to Swordfish
segments.summary: Reliable carrier torpedo attack emerged through years of aircraft, weapon, and release-system refinement rather than arriving fully formed.
segments.body: This passage cautions against projecting Second World War torpedo-bombing performance back into the early 1920s. Aircraft range, release accuracy, and torpedo reliability were all still developing when the Washington Treaty was negotiated. Dr. Clarke traces that learning curve from the Sopwith Cuckoo toward the Fairey Swordfish, highlighting wooden stabilizing fins and later tension-cable arrangements. The comparison explains why treaty delegates could not yet treat a carrier air group as a reliably ship-killing force.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the iterative technical changes that made later aerial torpedo drops reliable.

segments.title: How treaty wording permitted large new-build carriers
segments.summary: Article 9 encouraged conversion of existing capital ships but did not legally require the two permitted 33,000-ton carriers to be conversions.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke pauses over the permissive wording of Article 9 rather than treating the negotiators' expectation as a binding rule. The treaty said powers "may" use two ships otherwise due for scrapping to obtain the larger carrier allowance. That language encouraged economical conversion but did not require it. Britain, the United States, and Japan could therefore have built two purpose-designed 33,000-ton carriers from scratch in the 1920s, radically changing the technical baseline for naval aviation.

evidence.note: The transcript distinguishes the treaty's permissive legal language from the expectation that powers would convert existing hulls.

segments.title: Why purpose-built carriers used tonnage better
segments.summary: Comparing Furious, Eagle, Hermes, and Hōshō shows why purpose-built carriers could carry more aircraft for the same treaty tonnage.
segments.body: The video compares Furious, Eagle, Hermes, and Hōshō through tons per aircraft rather than displacement alone. Converted hulls carry the inherited compromises of their original design, while purpose-built ships can devote more of their footprint to hangars and aircraft handling. Hermes and Hōshō therefore appear markedly more tonnage-efficient than the larger conversions available to treaty planners. Under a total-tonnage treaty, that efficiency translates directly into more operational aircraft for the same legal allowance.

evidence.note: The comparison gives displacement and aircraft-capacity calculations for early converted and purpose-built carriers.

segments.title: Why the Royal Navy avoided routine deck parking
segments.summary: North Atlantic and North Sea weather made exposed aircraft an unreliable basis for Royal Navy carrier-capacity planning.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke explains why British and American carrier-capacity figures are not directly comparable when one navy routinely counts deck parks and the other does not. Royal Navy trials had shown that aircraft left exposed could be lost to heavy weather in the North Atlantic and North Sea. Although deck parking was practical in the Mediterranean, British planners wanted an air group that could survive deployment wherever the carrier was sent. Hangar footprint therefore mattered more to British capacity estimates than a maximum fair-weather deck load.

evidence.note: The transcript links Royal Navy deck-parking policy to aircraft losses in northern waters.

segments.title: Why larger carriers gain hangar efficiency
segments.summary: As a carrier grows, fixed systems consume a smaller proportion of its hangar footprint and leave more room for aircraft.
segments.body: This design explanation focuses on usable hangar footprint rather than displacement alone. Islands, funnel trunks, lifts, offices, and access routes occupy space on every carrier regardless of air-group size. On a larger hull, those fixed demands consume a smaller proportion of the available length and beam. The resulting efficiency helps a large purpose-built carrier support more aircraft and maintenance space than a proportionally scaled conversion.

evidence.note: The transcript explains how hangar footprint, islands, funnels, and lifts affect capacity as carrier size changes.

segments.title: Carrier aviation before the strike-carrier era
segments.summary: Early carrier value lay in scouting, counter-reconnaissance, spotting, and trade protection as much as in striking enemy ships.
segments.body: The Royal Navy’s interest in carrier numbers is presented as a response to trade protection, reconnaissance, and fleet-protection needs, not simply a search for ship-killing aviation. British assessments of Jutland and other operations gave German aerial reconnaissance an important role, strengthening the case for fighters that could suppress enemy scouting. Carrier aircraft could also find enemy forces and spot the fall of shot for battleships beyond direct visual range. Dr. Clarke describes the Blackburn Blackburn as a sensor node in a distributed weapon system: the aircraft supplied information while the battleship delivered the destructive effect.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses air-group size, trade protection, reconnaissance, and gunnery spotting.

segments.title: A proposed equitable carrier treaty
segments.summary: The alternate treaty carries a 10:10:7:4:4 capital-ship ratio into carrier limits while avoiding fractional ship allowances.
segments.body: The proposed settlement carries the video’s earlier 10:10:7:4:4 capital-ship ratio into carrier allocations. Dr. Clarke rejects fractional ship allowances because they add complexity and make equality depend on multiplying abstract ratios into much larger fleets. A 33,000-ton individual maximum comes directly from the historical treaty's exception, although applying it generally produces far larger totals than the actual agreement. The result is explicitly counterfactual rather than inevitable, but it tests whether transparent whole-ship ratios could better satisfy the strategic requirements of all five powers.

evidence.note: The transcript derives the alternative whole-ship ratio and explains the 33,000-ton individual limit.

segments.title: How carrier islands supported faster flight operations
segments.summary: British carrier islands combined command, aircrew organization, and smoke management while protecting scarce hangar space.
segments.body: Using HMS Hermes, Dr. Clarke explains why early British carriers devoted substantial volume to their island structures. Briefing and organizing pilots near the flight deck helped aircrew reach aircraft quickly when a launch was ordered. A tall island also carried funnel outlets high enough to reduce smoke interference over the deck. Experience with Argus, Eagle, Furious, Courageous, and Glorious taught the Royal Navy that funnel trunks passing through the hangar imposed a serious penalty on aircraft space.

evidence.note: The transcript connects island volume, pilot readiness, smoke clearance, and hangar-space efficiency.

segments.title: Continuous construction as industrial deterrence
segments.summary: A treaty that sustains shipbuilding capacity could leave every signatory more modern while reducing hopes of an easy challenge.
segments.body: The counterfactual broadens from carrier totals to the industrial effects of arms-control rules. Continued Allied construction would preserve shipyards, design experience, and successive generations of modern ships rather than allowing capacity to atrophy. Continued Japanese construction would also leave room for planners to hope that qualitative improvements could offset numerical inferiority, giving them a reason to remain within the system. Dr. Clarke argues that Germany and Italy would then confront a Royal Navy whose fleet and construction base looked much harder to overtake, changing the deterrent calculation before war began.

evidence.note: The transcript links continuing treaty construction to industrial capacity, fleet renewal, and deterrence.

segments.title: Conversion deadlines and carrier service lives
segments.summary: Staged conversion requirements would preserve existing investment while delaying unrestricted purpose-built carrier construction.
segments.body: The alternate rules require carriers completed through 1926 to be conversions and require at least two conversions in each navy's programme through 1930. This protects capital already committed to hulls and long-lead machinery while preventing an immediate race for wholly new 33,000-ton ships. Pre-treaty experimental carriers could retire from 1930, later conversions from 1934, and new builds would remain for at least eighteen years. The timetable uses replacement policy to spread technical change across several generations instead of trying to freeze carrier development.

evidence.note: The transcript specifies conversion deadlines, retirement dates, and an eighteen-year life for new carriers.

segments.title: Hōshō and Japan's early carrier advantage
segments.summary: Hōshō gave the Imperial Japanese Navy an unusually efficient early platform for reconnaissance and fleet support.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke singles out Hōshō as one of the most efficient early light-carrier designs despite the limitations expected of a first effort. At launch, her air group could provide reconnaissance and fleet support that the United States Navy would have had to take seriously. Her purpose-built layout gave Japan an early practical advantage over navies still relying heavily on conversions. The advantage was temporary as larger ships overtook her, but it demonstrates that early carrier competence did not develop evenly across navies.

evidence.note: The transcript assesses Hōshō's early reconnaissance value and design efficiency.

segments.title: The alternative carrier tonnage allocations
segments.summary: The proposed totals give Britain and the United States 330,000 tons each, Japan 231,000, and France and Italy 132,000 apiece.
segments.body: This concise recap translates the 10:10:7:4:4 ratio into national carrier-tonnage ceilings. Britain and the United States receive 330,000 tons each, Japan receives 231,000, and France and Italy receive 132,000 each. Every navy may build individual carriers up to 33,000 tons, subject to the earlier conversion and service-life rules. The numbers create choices about fleet count and ship size rather than prescribing a uniform carrier design.

evidence.note: The transcript states the five national totals and the individual maximum.

segments.title: How national carrier requirements shape fleet choices
segments.summary: Different oceans, bases, and fleet missions could lead Britain, the United States, Japan, France, and Italy toward different carrier sizes and numbers.
segments.body: The video treats tonnage as a force-structure choice rather than an automatic instruction to build the largest permitted ship. Italian concentration in the Mediterranean could make four large carriers decisive locally, while French commitments in the Mediterranean, Atlantic, and Far East rewarded a larger number of smaller ships. British three-fleet planning encouraged a carrier count divisible among operating areas, whereas the United States could concentrate on Atlantic and Pacific divisions and Japan could emphasize qualitative strength. These decisions also depended on what navies thought carriers were for: reconnaissance, air defence, spotting, and limited anti-submarine work dominated in the early 1920s before strike doctrine matured.

evidence.note: The transcript compares national allocations and explains evolving carrier missions.

segments.title: A British 27,500-ton carrier programme
segments.summary: A 27,500-ton British new build is presented as a compromise between carrier numbers, useful air groups, and the protection of later armoured designs.
segments.body: The proposed British programme keeps smaller Courageous-class conversions while using a 27,500-ton new design as a practical middle course. Dr. Clarke argues that this size could support a decent air group and useful protection without committing the fleet entirely to very large ships. He expects an early design to place its strength and armour deck below the hangar, preserving the hull if the upper carrier structure suffered bomb damage. Repeated construction at that displacement would give the Royal Navy several design generations before the Second World War, trading maximum individual size for a sustainable three-fleet force.

evidence.note: The transcript develops the 27,500-ton new build, its protection concept, and a sustained replacement programme.

segments.title: How carrier growth could reshape Fleet Air Arm control
segments.summary: A much larger carrier fleet would make Air Ministry control harder to reconcile with the Royal Navy's operational needs.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that sustained construction of numerous carriers would alter the institutional struggle over British naval aviation. He distinguishes the Royal Air Force as a service from the Air Ministry as the organization whose assumptions often constrained the Fleet Air Arm. One Air Ministry concept treated carriers as transports that could deliver land-based aircraft to the Far East and then send naval aircraft ashore to reinforce expeditionary air operations. That single-service logic risked leaving the fleet without the aircraft needed for reconnaissance, air defence, and strike work, making control of naval aviation an operational rather than merely bureaucratic question.

evidence.note: The transcript distinguishes RAF and Air Ministry positions and describes a carrier transport concept that would strip aircraft from the fleet.

segments.title: G3 conversions as a strike-carrier force
segments.summary: Converting four lightly started G3 hulls would trade total carrier numbers for a core of powerful strike carriers.
segments.body: The second British programme cancels the smaller Furious path and treats four lightly started G3 battlecruisers as treaty conversions. Long-lead items had already been ordered, so the ships could enter service relatively quickly despite little hull structure having been completed. Four roughly 30,000-ton conversions would reduce the total number of carriers but give the Royal Navy a persistent core of strike carriers alongside later 27,500-ton fleet carriers. Dr. Clarke checks the replacement timetable against the construction allowance and notes that the 1942 peak would require careful retirement timing to remain within the tonnage ceiling.

evidence.note: The transcript compares the G3 conversion programme's carrier count, strike strength, and replacement timing.

segments.title: What a G3 carrier conversion might enable
segments.summary: A G3 conversion could combine a large flight deck, substantial hangar space, flagship facilities, and the possibility of operating larger aircraft.
segments.body: Using Eagle and Ark Royal as design reference points, the video sketches how a G3 hull might be adapted without relocating its machinery. Its 260-metre length and broad hull could support a very large flight deck, multiple lifts, maintenance space, and a carrier-squadron flagship role. Funnel positions would shape an island farther aft than on historical Royal Navy carriers, making the proposal a design departure as well as a simple conversion. The discussion also considers whether a deck roughly 260 to 270 metres long might make twin-engine naval aircraft practical as engines improved, permitting greater payload and performance while acknowledging contemporary reliability debates.

evidence.note: The transcript examines G3 dimensions, conversion layout, and possible twin-engine operations.

segments.title: Treaty limits, security, and deterrence
segments.summary: The closing argument is that an arms-control system endures only when it provides security and an incentive for every power to keep it.
segments.body: The conclusion connects carrier rules to the larger political purpose of treaty design. It argues that peace cannot rest on freezing construction so tightly that states feel exposed while technology changes around them. Comparing the treaty era with the years after HMS Dreadnought, Dr. Clarke suggests that powers were denied opportunities to absorb new technology through normal construction and therefore lost confidence in their relative positions. A durable settlement instead needs a credible balance: enough preparedness to make aggression too costly, but not so much imbalance that fear encourages pre-emption or weakness invites opportunism. Treaty compliance lasts when each state believes its security and future advantage are better served by keeping the agreement than by abandoning it.

evidence.note: The transcript links treaty compliance, security, continued construction, and deterrence.
